questions.question_text: As of January 1, 2026, how many total pre-license education hours does Nebraska require?
questions.explanation: Effective January 1, 2026, Nebraska requires 96 total pre-license hours: 30 hours NE License Law, 30 hours Principles & Practices, 30 hours Property Management, and 6 hours Course #0003 Ethics.

questions.question_text: A candidate completes all 96 pre-license hours and passes the exam. What additional course must she take before license issuance?
questions.explanation: After completing the 96 pre-license hours and passing the exam, candidates must also complete a 3-hour Nebraska law course before the license is actually issued. This is separate from the 30-hour NE License Law course in the pre-license curriculum.

questions.question_text: A license applicant asks which agencies conduct the required background check. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Nebraska requires a background check through both the Nebraska State Patrol and the FBI, costing approximately $38. This dual-level check covers both state and national criminal history records.

questions.question_text: A candidate has completed all pre-license education but has no broker willing to sponsor her. Can she receive her license?
questions.correct_answer: No, a designated broker must sponsor her
distractors: Yes, she can hold an independent license
Yes, if she posts a surety bond
questions.explanation: Nebraska requires all new licensees to be sponsored by a designated broker before a license is issued. Without broker sponsorship, the candidate cannot obtain an active license regardless of education or exam completion.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is one of the four required pre-license courses under the 2026 Nebraska curriculum?
questions.explanation: The 2026 pre-license curriculum includes: 30-hr NE License Law, 30-hr Principles & Practices, 30-hr Property Management, and 6-hr Course #0003 Ethics. Property Management is a distinct 30-hour course in the required lineup.

questions.question_text: A new Nebraska licensee received her license on March 1. By when must she complete Course #7000?
questions.explanation: Course #7000 is a 12-hour post-license requirement that must be completed within 180 days of initial licensure. Failure to complete it within this window results in the license being placed on inactive status.

questions.question_text: How many continuing education hours must a Nebraska licensee complete per renewal cycle?
questions.explanation: Nebraska requires 18 hours of CE every 2-year renewal cycle. Of those, 12 must be NREC-approved courses, and 6 of those 12 must be designated 'R' (required) courses. Renewals are due November 30.

questions.question_text: A licensee with a property management license completes her standard 18 CE hours. Has she met all requirements?
questions.correct_answer: No, she needs 3 additional PM hours
distractors: No, she needs 6 additional PM hours
No, she needs 12 additional PM hours
questions.explanation: Property management licensees must complete 3 additional hours of PM-specific continuing education beyond the standard 18-hour CE requirement. This ensures PM licensees stay current on landlord-tenant law and management practices.

questions.question_text: A licensee took a non-R elective CE course in 2024. She wants to take the same course again in 2026 for credit. Is this allowed?
questions.correct_answer: No, duplicates earn no credit within 4 years
distractors: Yes, any course can be repeated
questions.explanation: Nebraska prohibits CE credit for duplicated course content within a 4-year period. The only exception is designated 'R' (required) courses, which may be repeated for credit. This policy encourages licensees to broaden their knowledge.

questions.question_text: When are Nebraska real estate license renewals due?
questions.explanation: Nebraska license renewals are due by November 30 of the renewal year. The renewal cycle is biennial (every 2 years). Licensees must complete all CE requirements before this deadline to maintain active status.

questions.question_text: A homeowner sells her own property without a license. Is this legal in Nebraska?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, owners may sell their own property
distractors: No, all sales require a license
Only if the sale is under $100,000
Only if she hires a licensed attorney
questions.explanation: Nebraska exempts property owners who sell their own real estate from licensing requirements. Other exemptions include attorneys acting within their practice, court-appointed persons, licensed auctioneers, and government officers acting in their official capacity.

questions.question_text: A licensed auctioneer conducts a real estate auction in Nebraska. Does she need a real estate license?
distractors: Yes, a broker license is required
Yes, a salesperson license is needed
questions.explanation: Licensed auctioneers are specifically exempt from Nebraska real estate licensing requirements when conducting real estate auctions. Other exemptions include property owners, attorneys in practice, court-appointed persons, and government officers.

questions.question_text: Which activity requires a Nebraska real estate license?
distractors: An owner leasing her own duplex
A sheriff conducting a foreclosure sale
questions.explanation: Property management performed for others is a licensed activity in Nebraska. Licensed activities include sales, auctions, property management, and leasing on behalf of others. Owners managing their own property, attorneys in practice, and court-appointed officials are exempt.

questions.question_text: An attorney negotiates a real estate sale as part of her law practice. Does she need a real estate license?
questions.correct_answer: No, attorneys in practice are exempt
distractors: Yes, all negotiators need a license
Only if she receives a commission
questions.explanation: Attorneys acting within the scope of their law practice are exempt from Nebraska real estate licensing. This exemption covers activities like negotiating sales, preparing contracts, and closing transactions as part of legal representation.

questions.question_text: A friend offers to help a neighbor sell his house for a flat fee. She is not licensed. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, she is performing licensed activities
distractors: Yes, if the fee is under $500
Yes, friends and family are exempt
Yes, flat fees avoid licensing rules
questions.explanation: Selling real estate on behalf of another person for compensation is a licensed activity in Nebraska regardless of the fee structure. Only specific statutory exemptions (owners, attorneys, auctioneers, court-appointed persons, government officers) apply.
